title: Google I/O 2025 – xr_zgAv0YoI
text:
[MUSIC PLAYING] JASON MAYES: Hello,
everyone, I'm Jason Mayes, Web
AI Lead at Google, for our teams that focus on
the art of running machine learning models client
side in a web browser. Today I've come to share a
story about why you should start investigating agentic behaviors
on-device within the browser in JavaScript to gain
extraordinary abilities for your existing web
applications, while also enabling privacy and lower
costs for your business as users' devices continue
to get more capable. In 2024, the Web AI
community progressed towards a smarter web that was
more powerful than ever before. Like this prototype
that enabled videos to literally watch
themselves for you, extracting useful video
frames in real time to grab moments that matter
automatically and could even work offline. But this, while smart, was
not an agentic behavior. So what's an agent? At its essence, an
agent is a system that can perform advanced
tasks autonomously on behalf of the user by breaking them
down into structured steps that it can then complete
without user intervention. An agent can dynamically
select and use exposed tools, potentially
many times in a loop, allowing it to interact
with the outside world, filling in any missing knowledge
it may need, or perform an external action
to then complete each substep beyond the
constraints of a core language model that it may be
using that ultimately is driving the agent's
internal thinking and logic. Let's dive into this tools
concept a bit deeper. While large language models
contain a vast amount of knowledge about
the world, if you ask one what the weather
is today in London, it would likely hallucinate
an answer that might be wrong. Instead, you can
expose a tool that fetches the current
weather for a given city that the language
model can be used to call with the inputs
it needs to get up to date information
about the weather, and then use that response
in its own contextual history to perform the task correctly. Tools allow the language model
to bridge the gap between itself
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – xr_zgAv0YoI
text:
to call with the inputs
it needs to get up to date information
about the weather, and then use that response
in its own contextual history to perform the task correctly. Tools allow the language model
to bridge the gap between itself and the outside world. An agent can potentially
use many of them to interact with various
external data and services, unlocking more powerful
actions that could be used to help it solve the goals set. The example shown here
illustrates potential tools you may expose for a
holiday planning agent. Common types of tools
include calling a function to perform some task, or taking
an action via an extension to use an API, or fetching
relevant contextual information from, say, a vector data store. Next, with regards to
language models used, this could actually be
one or more LLMs or even multi-modal models to understand
inputs beyond just text, but are all capable of following
instruction based reasoning. If multiple models are used,
the orchestration layer may decide which one to delegate
to for the current subtask being performed. Essentially, an agent can
plan and define subtasks that it needs to complete
to get a job done, choosing the appropriate tools
to use in each step whose output can be used
as extra context in its memory to then
reason what it needs to do in future steps in
order to complete them without constant
human intervention. Even cycling through
a chain of steps it chose to perform,
potentially multiple times, breaking the task down
further to eventually complete the request. So bringing this all
together, an agentic system is typically comprised of
one or more large language models, a memory implementation,
and one or more tools it can choose to call. These key subparts are
managed and coordinated by the agent runtime
or orchestration layer, if you will, that governs
how the agent takes in external inputs,
how it should loop to perform internal
reasoning, and even assisting in calling the correct
tools that the agent chooses
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – xr_zgAv0YoI
text:
by the agent runtime
or orchestration layer, if you will, that governs
how the agent takes in external inputs,
how it should loop to perform internal
reasoning, and even assisting in calling the correct
tools that the agent chooses to use based on structured
outputs from the language model itself. Or it can enable an ability
to ask follow up questions to the user as needed, and may
define valid stopping points to present results back
to the user at key steps. Now, complexity of the
orchestration layer will vary depending on
your implementation. So how does this
all tie into Web AI? Well, last year I
made a prediction that websites of
the future may need to be AI agent compatible,
such that you could talk to them naturally to
perform any of the tasks that they support to get
useful work done faster, without needing to learn
arbitrary user experiences on different websites. I also predicted that sites that
don't make it easy for agents to work with them may struggle
to compete in the agentic AI age, just like how
we had to support mobile versions of our
websites in the past to stay competitive when
the smartphone was invented. Today, I'll demonstrate
a Web AI prototype that puts these agentic
concepts into action to show you a glimpse of a
possible future of the internet itself, where websites are
designed to work interchangeably with agents and humans alike. I'll be using Google's 2
billion parameter Gemma 2 model, entirely
locally in the browser, powered by our MediaPipe
Web LLM library, to show you how you can
upgrade your existing site with agentic workflows
by reusing your existing code exposed as tools
that the agent can then use in a low friction manner. If all websites
embrace these concepts, we could enable a
smarter web for everyone. Everything you're
about to see is captured in real time on a five
generation old NVIDIA 1070 GPU, so these things will likely
only get faster with time too. In this demo, we're going
to simulate a flight search page similar to Google
Flights to illustrate
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – xr_zgAv0YoI
text:
captured in real time on a five
generation old NVIDIA 1070 GPU, so these things will likely
only get faster with time too. In this demo, we're going
to simulate a flight search page similar to Google
Flights to illustrate how this agent could work. Let's go ahead and
attempt to book a flight using the AI agent
microphone button exposed, but purposely not give
enough information. Hey, there. I'd like to go on
holiday with my friend. GEMMA 2: Where are you
departing from and when? Is that a one way trip? JASON MAYES: Notice how the
agent has already figured out that two people are likely going
on this trip in the JSON output on the left. However, it also
realizes that in order to use the website's
search tool, it needs more data to call that. So it asks us some
follow up questions too. Let's respond with what
it wanted, but also include more data
than it actually asks for to show how it
can adapt to anything the user throws at it. We'll be leaving
from San Francisco, but can you pick a
popular skiing destination in the French Alps
for us to go to? We'll leave on the 5th of
December and come back one week later. GEMMA 2: What is your preferred
destination in the French Alps? JASON MAYES: I don't mind. You pick a town that's good
for skiing in the region. So, as you can see, even
though I spoke quite a bit, it successfully extracted
the data information, figured out what one week later
actually means, and suggested the popular skiing
destination of Chamonix, which is a great choice. As it knows that it now
has enough information to perform a search, it goes
ahead and calls my search tool with the data. Now in this demo,
just for fun, I even got the LLM to generate
fictional results too just to show you how much of
knowledge of the world this tiny 2 billion parameter
model has even though it's not been fine tuned. So at no point in this demo did
I send any data to the Cloud. Although in real life,
a search would probably end up calling your Cloud
API to get the latest results from a real flights database.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – xr_zgAv0YoI
text:
been fine tuned. So at no point in this demo did
I send any data to the Cloud. Although in real life,
a search would probably end up calling your Cloud
API to get the latest results from a real flights database. And as you can see, it generated
some realistic flight options and even returned
European airlines. All right. So we've got some results. But let's show how
versatile this is. You know what? We had a change of plans and
an extra friend is coming and we decided to
go to Tokyo instead. As this is a special trip,
let's make those seats business class, baby, and
change the dates to a better time in autumn. So here I'm hoping it increments
the passenger count to three, sets seats to business, and
destination should become Tokyo. And I'm hoping for some
dates in the September to November time range. And sure enough, it
seems to have worked. Now, as I said, everything here
is running entirely locally on my laptop's GPU, meaning that
you could talk to it all day long and there'll be no
inference costs involved. The only cost here is
the initial download of a language model based on
its size and the CDM provider that you're using,
which is a one off, and it's cached for
future page loads. Speaking of caching, I've
created a useful caching proxy library available
on the link shown that you can use when
fetching large generative AI models from a server in
just a few lines of code. This library makes it easy
to fetch large binary files, and then shards them
into smaller chunks to fit within the limits
of client side caching APIs and reconstructs the
binary blob as a data URL so you can pass it to any
library of your choice at runtime, as if it were
fetching a remote URL. And this is how easy it is to
interact with existing website functions that you've
already written. As you've seen, this can
lead to an experience that's much more natural for
the user versus having to learn a new user experience
for every website they view. Not to mention, even a
visually impaired person
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – xr_zgAv0YoI
text:
As you've seen, this can
lead to an experience that's much more natural for
the user versus having to learn a new user experience
for every website they view. Not to mention, even a
visually impaired person could use this interface
too, using just their voice, making the website
much more accessible. Pretty cool, right? So if you're wondering how
this all comes together, the diagram shows
the key dataflow for what I've made in the
web app that you just saw. The key thing to take note of
is that the agent orchestration logic in yellow just attaches
to your existing business logic shown in
green, and you can choose what functions
from your existing web app to expose, to
provide it with tools it can use to get jobs done. On the page load, the web app
downloads the large language model I plan to use
from Cloud Storage to power the agentic experience. In this case, I'm using Google's
MediaPipe Web LLM library to load the Gemma 2 2
billion parameter model and run it entirely
locally in the browser. Once loaded, I can
register functions that already exist in the
existing business application logic shown in green as a tool
that the agent controller then knows how to use
to call as needed to perform any of the tasks
that the website supports. Let's dive into this
part a little deeper. In my implementation,
the agent is flexibly defined in the side panel
on the right, as shown here, to make it easy to edit on
the fly for experimentation at runtime. As you can see, I
define a persona along with the functions
I want to expose as tools, just by using regular
JSDoc comments that I've already got in
my production code base and could likely
be autogenerated to some degree in the future. The agent is then aware of their
existence to use as it needs. Now the core pieces
are connected, the web app is ready to go. A user can use their
microphone to talk to the configured
agent in a natural way, using a lightweight speech
input layer that I wrote. This speech is transcribed
and then routed
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – xr_zgAv0YoI
text:
the web app is ready to go. A user can use their
microphone to talk to the configured
agent in a natural way, using a lightweight speech
input layer that I wrote. This speech is transcribed
and then routed to the agent controller
logic shown in yellow, and then it uses the loaded LLM
with the defined agent persona and the exposed tools that
are available to use along with the current user input. The LLM is then able
to generate a response in a defined,
structured JSON format that the agent orchestration
logic can interpret and deal with, such as asking follow
up questions, if needed, or calling a tool,
until it has enough data to do what's asked of it. In this case, to book a flight. In my example, a
text to speech engine is also used to speak to the
user if follow up questions are required. Now, in case you're
wondering how I knew what JSON
structure to use, I specified this in the
agent's configuration, whereby I gave example JSON
structured output that I would like it to adhere to. So that my orchestration
layer can route its request to the right tool as needed
via common known interface to expect at runtime. I can change all of
this at any point to experiment with
different personas, or work with other
APIs or functions that I've exposed if I
wanted to, making it very easy to explore future options. Finally, when enough
information is obtained to perform
the desired task, the agent can call the search
tool exposed by the business application to actually find
possible flights that match and bring those results back. The results are rendered to the
user interface via the existing logic in the web app
itself that would already have been handling search
results in the non-AI version of the site. So you're just reusing code that
you've already got in production Once the results are
rendered, the user can make a new request, maybe to
apply further filters or change destinations as they desire,
kicking off the whole process all over again. Except this time, the
agent has a memory
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – xr_zgAv0YoI
text:
Once the results are
rendered, the user can make a new request, maybe to
apply further filters or change destinations as they desire,
kicking off the whole process all over again. Except this time, the
agent has a memory of the prior conversation, so
it doesn't have to ask so many follow up questions. OK. So with that in mind, I can
now reuse the same logic with a different
web application that has different exposed tools. In the example here, I made
a Web AI DJ in just one day by reusing the
framework that you just saw that works with public
APIs for music services to show how versatile
this can be. I even upgraded the
text to speech engine to use a Web AI model known
as [? QoQoro ?] for a more natural sounding voice. You can try it for
yourself on the link shown. So as you've seen, Web AI
is the art of using machine learning models client
side in a web browser, running on your own device's
processor, graphics card, or even NPU using JavaScript
and surrounding web technologies like WebAssembly, WebGPU, or
emerging standards like WebNN for acceleration. And in some cases,
the base AI model may even be built into
the browser itself and exposed to
JavaScript developers via emerging APIs for
common tasks like prompting, summarization, and translation. So do check out Chrome's
talks at Google I/O this year to hear their updates. Now, all of this is different
from Cloud AI, whereby, in that case, the model would
execute on the server side and be accessed via some
sort of API instead, which means you'd need an active
internet connection to talk to the Cloud API at all times
to provide those advanced capabilities. Right now, generative AI in the
browser is in its early stages, but as hardware
continues to evolve with more RAM available
to both CPU and GPU, we shall continue to see newer,
more powerful models ported to run in browser
on your device, enabling businesses to
reimagine what you can actually do on a web page, especially
for industry or task specific situations. In fact, we could see
smaller large language
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – xr_zgAv0YoI
text:
to run in browser
on your device, enabling businesses to
reimagine what you can actually do on a web page, especially
for industry or task specific situations. In fact, we could see
smaller large language models in the 2 to 9
billion parameter range be lower tuned for a specific
purpose via the Cloud, and then run on
consumer hardware to provide agentic behaviors
like you saw today. And we're now at the start
of a new era that can really enhance your existing web
presence for the services and products you're
already providing. And the time is now
to start exploring how you could apply Web AI
agents to your industry, augmenting the strengths of
client side Web AI and Cloud AI together. You could consider using hybrid
approaches with your smaller local models today. For example, imagine
you've got a vector database of domain specific
knowledge in the Cloud. You could pair that as a
tool with a Web AI agent on the client side to
provide critical context to answer a user's
request, allowing you to get a more advanced task
done that otherwise the smaller model may not have
known how to do. And by using both Web AI
and Cloud AI together, it allows you to get the key
benefits of both ecosystems. Now then, I'd just
like to end by saying there are very few
opportunities in one's life to be at the beginning
of a new era like this. I, for one, remember when
the internet came out for the first time. Yes, I am that old. And agentic Web AI has
the same feels as that, only magnified 1,000x. I personally believe
that in the future, we will build AI compatible
websites like you saw today, whereby you've got the option
to just talk to them naturally via text or voice to
perform any of the tasks that they support
using an AI agent. What if every website had a
publicly accessible file that's specifically written so that
an agent can understand what tools your service can provide? That way, instead of the user
having to manually figure out some arbitrary workflow
or user experience,
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – xr_zgAv0YoI
text:
What if every website had a
publicly accessible file that's specifically written so that
an agent can understand what tools your service can provide? That way, instead of the user
having to manually figure out some arbitrary workflow
or user experience, the agent can use that knowledge
to get the job done in seconds. Now, the browser is one of
the best places to do this, as you've already signed
in to all the services you love and use daily, enabling
you to use tools from all of those services in one place. I believe that sites that
are not AI agent compatible may be out of fashion
pretty fast, just like when we moved from desktop
to mobile first, and we're now entering the
age where AI agents are growing in popularity. So I hope in this
talk, I've been able to provide you
with a tiny glimpse into the future as to why you
should start investigating Web AI today, because
everyone watching here has a chance of a
lifetime to shape the future of the
industries that you work in for this fast growing
client side space. And I hope all of you will
join me on this adventure. So do tag me in anything
you make at your company, or if you've got suggestions
or questions around any of the technologies
mentioned in this talk. You can connect with
me on social media or learn more using the
links on this slide. And of course, remember
to use the Web AI hashtag if you make anything
worth sharing so our community can find it. And with that, thank you,
and I'll see you next time. [MUSIC PLAYING]
speaker: unknown
